question_title: Will Japan's retrial reform bill (amendment to the Code of Criminal Procedure) pass the House of Representatives before May 31, 2026?
summary: The retrial reform bill was submitted to the House of Representatives on May 15, 2026, but NHK confirmed that formal deliberation (審議入り) is scheduled to begin only on May 26 — giving just 4 working days (May 26-29) before the May 30 Saturday deadline. The bill is classified as an Important and Broad Bill (重要広範議案) requiring PM attendance at questioning, multiple committee deliberation sessions, committee vote, and then a plenary vote. Completing this entire legislative process in 4 working days would be essentially unprecedented in Japanese Diet history, even with the LDP's commanding 316-seat supermajority. Crucially, there is no evidence the government is targeting passage by May 30 — their stated goal is enactment during the full session ending July 17. The 5% probability accounts for the small chance that behind-the-scenes agreements, procedural shortcuts, or earlier-than-reported deliberation progress could accelerate the timeline beyond what public reporting indicates.

factors.description: Deliberation only begins May 26 (confirmed by NHK), giving just 4 working days (Tue-Fri) before the May 30 Saturday deadline. Passing an Important and Broad Bill from first plenary introduction to plenary passage in 4 days would be essentially unprecedented in Japanese Diet history.

Bill classified as 重要広範議案 (Important and Broad Bill) requiring PM attendance at questioning, which mandates extensive procedural steps including趣旨説明, 代表質疑, multiple committee sessions, and plenary vote.
Government's own stated timeline is passage during the Diet session (by July 17), with no indication of urgency for May 30 specifically. Justice Minister said 'aiming for passage this session.'
Opposition parties submitted a counter-proposal on May 15, signaling expectation of extended deliberation and negotiation, not a 4-day rush.
LDP has overwhelming 316/465 supermajority (confirmed by 2026 election results), giving them ability to force votes whenever they want — if they wanted to rush, they could procedurally do so.
Bill has broad cross-party support in principle (cross-party league with over half of Diet members), which could reduce obstruction if fast-tracking were attempted.

reasoning:
## Forecast: Will Japan's retrial reform bill pass the House of Representatives before May 31, 2026?

**Probability: 5% (NO is very likely)**

### Key Reasoning

The critical constraint is **timing**. NHK confirmed that formal House of Representatives deliberation (審議入り) on this bill is scheduled to begin **May 26** — but the deadline is **May 30** (a Saturday, when the Diet normally doesn't meet), leaving only **4 working days** (May 26-29).

**Why this is nearly impossible in the timeframe:**
- The bill is classified as a **重要広範議案** (Important and Broad Bill), requiring PM Takaichi's personal attendance at plenary questioning
- Standard procedure demands: plenary introduction → committee referral → multiple committee hearings → committee vote → plenary vote
- Even fast-tracked bills of this classification typically require **1-2 weeks minimum** of committee deliberation
- Completing all stages in 4 days would be **essentially unprecedented** in Japanese Diet history

**Why the government isn't rushing:**
- The stated goal is passage during the **current Diet session** (ending July 17), not by May 30
- The opposition submitted a counter-proposal, expecting extended debate
- Nikkei noted "uncertainty" about whether the bill would pass even by session end

**What could make me wrong:**
- Behind-the-scenes party agreements to fast-track
- Deliberation that started earlier than reported at committee level
- A rare Saturday Diet session on May 30

The LDP's 316/465 supermajority gives them the power to force votes, but there's no evidence they intend to rush this particular bill on this timeline.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will Japan's retrial reform bill (amendment to the Code of Criminal Procedure) pass the House of Representatives before May 31, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43567)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if, before May 31, 2026 (Japan Standard Time), the plenary of Japan's House of Representatives (衆議院本会議) passes the Cabinet-submitted bill to amend the Code of Criminal Procedure concerning the retrial system (the bill approved by Cabinet on May 15, 2026). Resolution will be determined by checking the official House of Representatives bill tracking page for the current (221st) Diet session at https://www.shugiin.go.jp/internet/itdb_gian.nsf/html/gian/menu.htm — specifically, the entry for the relevant 閣法 (cabinet bill) titled "刑事訴訟法の一部を改正する法律案" must show a status indicating passage by the House of Representatives (e.g., "衆議院で可決" / "参議院で審議中" / "成立"). If the official Shugiin page is unavailable, reporting from [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) confirming the House of Representatives plenary vote may be used. The question resolves NO if no such House of Representatives plenary passage has occurred by 23:59 JST on May 30, 2026.
fine_print: Only passage of the Cabinet-submitted government bill counts; passage of the opposition counterproposal alone does not. If the government bill is amended during deliberation and the amended version is passed by the House of Representatives plenary, that counts as YES. Committee-level passage (法務委員会) without a plenary vote does not count. If the bill is withdrawn before a vote, the question resolves NO.

input.anchor: Base rate for a major Japanese government bill passing the House of Representatives within 4 working days of deliberation starting: near zero historically. Even fast-tracked bills take 1-2 weeks minimum for committee deliberation on Important and Broad Bills.
input.assessment:
The question asks whether the retrial reform bill will pass the House of Representatives plenary by May 30, 2026. The critical constraint is timing: NHK confirmed deliberation begins May 26, and the deadline is May 30 (a Saturday, so effectively May 29 is the last working day). This gives only 4 working days.

For an Important and Broad Bill (重要広範議案) in Japan's Diet, the procedure requires: (1) plenary趣旨説明 with PM questioning, (2) committee referral, (3) multiple committee sessions of deliberation, (4) committee vote, (5) plenary vote. Even under the most aggressive possible schedule, this would require telescoping what normally takes 2-4 weeks into 4 days.

The LDP has the votes (316/465 supermajority) to force passage whenever they want, but there's no evidence they intend to rush this bill. The government's stated goal is passage during the session (by July 17), giving them nearly 2 months. The opposition counter-proposal signals expected deliberation, not a rubber-stamp process.

The only YES scenario would be if deliberation somehow started earlier than May 26 (perhaps at committee level), or if the Diet held a rare Saturday session on May 30. Neither appears to be happening based on current evidence. The bill status page shows "衆議院で審議中" which means it's in the House but formal deliberation hasn't meaningfully progressed.

I estimate ~5% probability, almost entirely accounting for the possibility that I'm wrong about the start date or some procedural shortcut exists I'm not aware of.
input.calibration_notes: The anchor is very strong here — the timeline constraint is nearly physical. Even with unlimited political will, Japanese Diet procedures have minimum time requirements for Important and Broad Bills. My 5% estimate accounts for unknown unknowns (maybe deliberation started earlier than reported, maybe a procedural shortcut exists, maybe the Diet meets on Saturday).
input.key_uncertainties: 1. Whether deliberation actually starts May 26 or could have started earlier at committee level; 2. Whether any procedural shortcuts exist for fast-tracking; 3. Whether the Diet could meet on Saturday May 30 for a special session
input.tool_audit: research() was very helpful — provided comprehensive timeline with NHK source confirming May 26 deliberation start date. web_search confirmed LDP supermajority of 316 seats. fetch_url on Shugiin confirmed bill status as '衆議院で審議中'. The NHK article URL was not directly fetchable but the research agent had already extracted the key information.
input.update_triggers: 1. Reports of committee deliberation starting before May 26 would increase probability significantly; 2. News of a Diet calendar showing plenary vote scheduled for May 29 would make it near-certain; 3. Reports of an agreement between parties to expedite this specific bill
input.process_reflection: The research tool worked well for this question, providing comprehensive legislative history. The web search and fetch tools complemented it effectively. The main challenge was confirming very recent Diet scheduling details — Japanese Diet committee schedules change frequently and the most current information may not be online. The code sandbox was useful for calendar analysis.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Systematic Review

### Factors verified against trace

**Factor 1 (deliberation begins May 26, 4 working days):** NHK article title "再審制度見直し 刑訴法改正案 26日に衆議院で審議入りへ" confirms May 26 start. Code execution verified May 26 = Tuesday, May 30 = Saturday, yielding 4 working days (May 26-29). Grounded.

**Factor 2 (重要広範議案 classification):** Research entry key_facts explicitly states the bill is classified as Important and Broad Bill requiring PM attendance. Grounded.

**Factor 3 (government timeline targets session end, not May 30):** Nikkei quoted Justice Minister Hiraguchi saying "今国会で成立できるよう" (aiming for passage this session). Diet session confirmed ending July 17. No source shows any urgency for May 30. Grounded.

**Factor 4 (opposition counter-proposal May 15):** Jiji Press article "Japan Opposition Submit Retrial Reform Counterproposal" confirms three parties submitted on May 15. Grounded.

**Factor 5 (LDP 316/465 supermajority):** Wikipedia on the 2026 election and multiple news sources (Al Jazeera, Nippon.com, Japan Times) confirm 316 seats and two-thirds supermajority. Grounded.

**Factor 6 (cross-party support, over half of Diet members):** Research entry states "cross-party Retrial Reform Diet Members' League was formed in March 2024 with over half of all Diet members joining." Grounded.

### Double-counting check
Factors 1 and 2 are related (timeline constraint + procedural requirements that make it binding) but draw on distinct evidence — calendar facts vs. bill classification. Not true double-counting.

### Wrong-direction check
All logit signs are consistent with their descriptions. Positive factors (5, 6) correctly push toward YES; negative factors (1-4) correctly push toward NO.

### Resolution criteria engagement
The agent fetched the official Shugiin bill tracking page and confirmed status "衆議院で審議中" (under deliberation). The factors directly address the timeline constraint (May 30 deadline) and procedural requirements for House plenary passage. Good alignment with resolution criteria.

### Missing resolution criteria
Resolution criteria are present and clearly specified. The agent attempted and succeeded in recovering the bill status from the official source. No issue.

### Diet session numbering
Minor discrepancy: research tool produced "217th Diet session" while Shugiin website says "221st" (different counting conventions — 特別国会 vs. 通常国会). The agent noted this confusion but it doesn't affect the forecast.

### Counterargument quality
The counterargument raises specific, concrete scenarios: LDP supermajority forcing votes, "審議入り" possibly referring to committee-level start, behind-the-scenes 国対 agreements, and the 法務委員会 already meeting on May 22. These are substantive alternative scenarios, not token gestures. Adequate.

### Confidence assessment
0.75 confidence is reasonable given strong evidence (NHK confirmed timeline, official bill status, procedural requirements) with genuine residual uncertainty about behind-the-scenes deals or procedural shortcuts.

### Probability assessment
The mechanical factor sum yields ~0.9-1.2% probability. The agent adjusted upward to 5% for unknown unknowns, which is a reasonable buffer. My independent estimate based on the evidence would be 3-6%, fully consistent with the agent's 5%. The evidence overwhelmingly supports a low probability: deliberation hasn't started, only 4 working days available, the bill requires extensive procedure as an Important and Broad Bill, and the government itself isn't targeting May 30.

### Tool audit
The agent says the NHK URL "was not directly fetchable" — consistent with the 50% error rate (1/2) on fetch_url calls shown in metrics. No disagreement.

### Conclusion
All factual claims are well-grounded in the research trace. No hallucinated evidence, no double-counting, no wrong-direction factors, no contradictory assessment, and the probability estimate is well-calibrated to the evidence. The 5% probability appropriately reflects the near-impossibility of passing an Important and Broad Bill through all required stages in 4 working days when deliberation hasn't even started, while leaving a small buffer for unforeseen procedural shortcuts.
